chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ Extreme Potency: The unidentified toxin is exceptionally potent and heavily concentrated throughout the entire plant (leaves, stems, petals, buds, and pollen)2. ◦ Lethal in Minuscule Doses: A toxic, potentially fatal dose can be reached simply by a cat ingesting 1 to 3 petals or leaves, grooming a few grains of pollen off their fur, or even taking a few sips of water from a vase that held the cu
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ Renal Destruction: The unknown toxin specifically targets the cat's kidneys, concentrating in the proximal convoluted tubule2. ◦ Cellular Necrosis: It causes acute tubular necrosis (the premature death of cells in the kidneys), which rapidly progresses to severe and irreversible acute kidney failure23. ◦ Systemic Cascade: The initial mechanism triggers vomiting, lethargy, and loss of ap
chemicals.name: Unknown / Unidentified toxic principle

description: True lilies (Lilium spp.), including Easter Lily, Tiger Lily, Stargazer Lily, Asiatic Lily, and others in the Lilium genus, are among the most lethal plants for cats. Virtually every part — petals, leaves, stems, pollen, and even vase water — is highly nephrotoxic. The specific toxic compound has not been identified, but its effect is swift and devastating: acute kidney failure with high mortality if untreated.
emergencyNote: Induction of Vomiting (Emesis) and Activated Charcoal Administration
name: Lilies
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: unknown nephrotoxic substance specific to Lilium (and Hemerocallis/Daylily); toxic to cats but not dogs or humans
Mechanism: acute tubular necrosis — rapid, irreversible destruction of kidney tissue within 24–72 hours
Symptoms Phase 1 (0–2h): vomiting, lethargy, drooling; Phase 2 (24–72h): kidney failure with decreased/absent urination, dehydration, seizures, coma
Even licking pollen off fur or drinking vase water can cause fatal poisoning
True lily exposure is a veterinary emergency — early IV fluid therapy significantly improves survival; do not wait for symptoms to develop

symptoms.name: Vomiting, Diarrhea, Drooling (Salivation), Abdominal Pain, and Inappetence (Anorexia or Loss of Appetite)
symptoms.notes: These are usually the first warning signs to appear3. The initial vomiting may temporarily cease after a few hours, but it will frequently return as the pet becomes dehydrated and renal failure begins to set in24. 2. Generalized Lethargy and Weakness
symptoms.onset: Rapid; typically within 1 to 4 hours of ingestion

symptoms.name: Lethargy, Depression, Generalized Weakness, and Recumbency (inability to rise)
symptoms.notes: A cat will quickly stop eating and appear noticeably sluggish5. As the condition worsens, the cat will become progressively weaker leading up to death2. 3. Acute Kidney Failure
symptoms.onset: Initial lethargy and depression appear within a few hours (1 to 12 hours)35.

symptoms.name: Acute Kidney Failure (characterized by Increased Thirst, Dehydration, Polyuria, and Anuria)
symptoms.notes: The toxin causes acute tubular necrosis in the kidneys6. Between 12 and 30 hours, the cat may exhibit increased thirst and excessive urination (polyuria)12. As the kidneys fail, this progresses to a complete cessation of urine production (anuria)24. Bloodwork will show toxic indications through elev
symptoms.onset: Delayed; kidney failure typically occurs within 36 to 72 hours (1 to 3 days)

symptoms.name: Seizures / Tremors
symptoms.notes: While generally considered an uncommon sign of lily toxicity, seizures can occur in very severe cases as a secondary complication2. They are triggered by substantial uremia, which is the dangerous buildup of waste products in the blood due to the kidneys shutting down
symptoms.onset: Delayed; typically occurs in the late stages of toxicity as kidney failure progresses

treatments.description: If the ingestion was recent, the veterinarian will administer medication to induce vomiting to expel any remaining plant material from the stomach. This is followed by administering a dose of activated charcoal, often along with a saline cathartic, to bind to the toxins in the gastrointestinal tract and prevent them from being absorbed into the bloodstream1....
treatments.name: Induction of Vomiting (Emesis) and Activated Charcoal Administration
treatments.notes: This must be performed as soon as possible (within hours of ingestion) and only if the cat is neurologically stable. Delayed treatment is associated with a very poor prognosis23. After decontamination, the vet may give an antiemetic to stop further vomiting3. 2. Aggressive Intravenous (IV) Fluid The

treatments.description: The cat is hospitalized and given intravenous fluids at an aggressive rate—typically 2 to 3 times their normal daily maintenance requirement—for 48 to 72 hours3.
treatments.name: IV Fluid Diuresis (Renoprotection)
treatments.notes: This is widely considered the most critical, viable treatment to protect the kidneys (renoprotection) and flush the toxins out of the system34. During this time, the veterinarian will perform baseline bloodwork (CBC, chemistry, and urinalysis) and monitor the cat's renal values every 24 hours to eva

treatments.description: The vet may administer gastroprotectants to soothe the stomach lining. If the cat begins to experience oliguria (a dangerous decrease in urine output), the vet may administer aggressive medications like mannitol or furosemide to stimulate the kidneys and force urine production3.
treatments.name: Gastroprotectants and Diuretics
treatments.notes: Gastroprotectants are specifically used if the cat develops secondary gastritis due to azotemia (a toxic buildup of waste products in the blood from failing kidneys)3. 4. Dialysis

treatments.description: A medical procedure that artificially filters waste products and toxins from the blood when the kidneys can no longer do so themselves3.
treatments.name: Peritoneal Dialysis or Hemodialysis
treatments.notes: This is a drastic, last-resort intervention considered only if the cat develops anuria (a complete cessation of urine production), indicating that the kidneys have fully shut down
